research_question: How can we design a training-light, reference-based anomaly detection framework that reliably localizes diverse defects in ultra-high-resolution (6559×6559) grayscale semiconductor images by comparing only defect-free normal reference patches, while maintaining low false-positive rates and sub-minute inference under domain shift from style, brightness, and process noise variations?
background: Semiconductor wafer and die inspection demands pixel-level defect detection across extremely large field-of-view images, yet actual defect samples are rare and highly diverse, making supervised approaches impractical. Reference-based anomaly detection using only normal (defect-free) patches has emerged as a promising paradigm, leveraging pretrained feature extractors and memory-bank methods to avoid costly labeling, but scaling these approaches to ultra-high-resolution images under strict latency and robustness requirements remains an open challenge. Addressing this gap is critical for enabling automated, high-throughput quality control in advanced semiconductor manufacturing where equipment drift, lot-to-lot variation, and subtle sub-pixel defects continuously challenge existing inspection systems.

image_analysis.input_characteristics: 6559×6559 single-channel (grayscale) semiconductor source images with fine-grained repetitive textures, process noise, and potential brightness/contrast variations across lots and equipment; reference patches are 256×256 or 512×512 defect-free normal crops from potentially different style/contrast distributions.
image_analysis.target_characteristics: Per-pixel anomaly score map (same spatial extent as the source image) highlighting defective regions, ultimately thresholded into bounding boxes or binary segmentation masks indicating defect locations and types.
image_analysis.visual_gap: Source images are orders of magnitude larger than reference patches, may exhibit global style/brightness mismatches, and contain extremely small or subtle defects embedded in complex repetitive semiconductor patterns that closely resemble normal texture variations.

problem_analysis.core_problem: Detecting and localizing rare, diverse, and often subtle defects in ultra-high-resolution semiconductor images using only defect-free reference patches, without supervised training, under strict inference time and false-positive constraints.
current_limitations: Standard anomaly detection methods (PatchCore, SPADE, PaDiM) are designed for moderate-resolution images and do not natively handle 6559×6559 inputs, causing memory and latency bottlenecks.
Pretrained feature extractors (ImageNet-based) are optimized for RGB natural images, leading to domain gap when applied to grayscale semiconductor textures with fine repetitive patterns.
Memory-bank approaches suffer from scalability issues as the number of stored normal embeddings grows, increasing retrieval time and memory footprint.
Simple pixel-level or template-matching comparisons are highly sensitive to brightness, contrast, and alignment variations, producing excessive false positives.
Existing registration-then-compare pipelines struggle with non-rigid local deformations and process-induced pattern variability across different lots and equipment.
Few methods jointly optimize for both detection sensitivity of very small defects and suppression of false alarms under domain shift without retraining.
problem_analysis.why_difficult: The problem sits at the intersection of multiple hard challenges: (1) ultra-high resolution requires efficient patch decomposition, parallel processing, and seamless merging without boundary artifacts; (2) the extreme scarcity and diversity of defect types precludes supervised or even few-shot defect-aware training; (3) domain shift from equipment, process, and lot variations means the normal distribution is non-stationary, demanding style-invariant or quickly adaptable representations; (4) defects range from large scratches to sub-pixel particle contaminations, requiring multi-scale sensitivity; and (5) strict inference time (< 1 minute) and single-GPU constraints prohibit brute-force ensemble or exhaustive search strategies.

in_scope: Patch-based splitting and merging strategies for ultra-high-resolution grayscale image processing
Reference-based anomaly detection using only normal (defect-free) patches
Feature extraction and matching using pretrained or lightly adapted backbone networks
Memory-bank and nearest-neighbor-based anomaly scoring methods
Style/brightness/noise normalization or invariant feature learning for cross-condition robustness
Efficient inference pipeline design for single-GPU deployment under 1-minute constraint
Anomaly map generation with bounding box or mask output
Evaluation under false-positive-centric metrics and domain shift scenarios
Few-shot or zero-shot adaptation to new lots/equipment via reference patch update
out_scope: End-to-end training of large foundation models (e.g., full fine-tuning of ViT-Large or diffusion models)
Supervised defect classification or segmentation requiring large annotated defect datasets
RGB or multi-spectral image processing pipelines
Defect root-cause analysis or process control feedback
Real-time video stream processing (focus is on single-image batch inspection)

primary: reference-based anomaly detection semiconductor defect inspection

quantitative: False Positive Rate on normal images ≤ 1% (ideally ≤ 0.5%)
False Alarm per normal image ≤ 0.5 on average
Anomalous area ratio on normal images ≤ 0.1%
Mean Absolute Score Shift under style/noise perturbation ≤ 0.05 (normalized score)
Pixel-level AUROC for defect localization ≥ 0.95 where ground truth is available
Image-level AUROC ≥ 0.97 where ground truth is available
Inference time per 6559×6559 source image ≤ 60 seconds on single GPU
Peak GPU memory usage ≤ 16 GB (single consumer/workstation GPU)
qualitative: Detected defects should be well-localized with tight bounding boxes or accurate masks without excessive over-segmentation
System should adapt to new lots or equipment by simply adding or swapping normal reference patches without retraining
Anomaly maps should be interpretable, clearly distinguishing true defects from normal texture variations
Pipeline should be modular, allowing independent upgrade of feature extractor, matching strategy, or normalization module
Robust and stable anomaly scores across repeated runs and minor input variations

constraints: Input source image is 6559×6559 single-channel grayscale
Reference images are defect-free normal patches of 256×256 or 512×512 or smaller
Source and reference images may differ in resolution, height, and width
Defect annotations are extremely limited or completely unavailable
Training must be minimized: normal-only, few-shot, or training-light approaches preferred
Must run on a single GPU with limited compute resources (e.g., single RTX 3090/4090 or A100)
Full inspection of one source image must complete within 1 minute
Must be robust to inter-lot and inter-equipment style, contrast, brightness, and noise variations
End-to-end fine-tuning of very large foundation models is discouraged
Must not excessively increase false positives; false alarm control is a primary design priority
target_metrics: False Positive Rate (FPR) on Normal Images
Anomalous Area Ratio on Normal Images
Score Stability: Mean Absolute Score Shift under Style/Noise Perturbation
Threshold Robustness (AUROC sensitivity to threshold selection)
Pixel-level AUROC / PRO (Per-Region Overlap) where ground truth available
Image-level AUROC where ground truth available
expected_challenges: Efficiently processing and merging anomaly scores across thousands of overlapping patches from a 6559×6559 image while avoiding boundary artifacts, ensuring spatial consistency, and meeting the sub-minute inference constraint on a single GPU.

Achieving robust feature matching between source patches and normal reference patches despite significant domain shift from brightness, contrast, process noise, and equipment variations—without retraining—while keeping false positives extremely low and not missing subtle or very small defects.

Bridging the domain gap between pretrained (ImageNet RGB) feature extractors and grayscale semiconductor textures with highly repetitive micro-patterns, where standard features may lack discriminative power for subtle anomalies and lightweight domain adaptation or specialized normalization strategies are needed without full model fine-tuning.

input.topic: AI-based defect detection in high-resolution grayscale semiconductor images using normal-only reference patches
input.details: Develop an anomaly detection system that automatically localizes defects in 6559x6559 grayscale semiconductor source images by leveraging only defect-free reference patches (256x256, 512x512 or smaller), employing reference-based feature matching, memory bank approaches, and pretrained feature extractors to minimize training and labeling requirements.
input.problem_definition: The source images are ultra-high-resolution (6559x6559) grayscale semiconductor images that cannot be processed at once, requiring patch-based splitting and merging strategies. Actual defect data is extremely scarce with diverse defect types, making supervised detection infeasible due to high labeling costs and insufficient defect samples. Additionally, variations in image style, brightness, process noise, and equipment differences cause normal patterns to appear differently, making simple rule-based or pixel-level comparison approaches prone to high false positives while very small defects and subtle texture differences must still be detected under strict inference time constraints.
input.desired_outcome: The system should reliably detect defect candidates using only normal reference patches and generate an anomaly map or defect score map for each source image, ultimately presenting defect locations as bounding boxes or masks. It must maintain robust performance under style and noise variations, complete full inspection of one source image within 1 minute, and adapt to new lots, equipment, or styles without retraining or with only minimal addition of normal reference patches.
input.constraints: Input is 6559x6559 grayscale source image, Reference images are defect-free normal patches (256x256 or 512x512 or smaller), Input and output images may differ in height and width, Defect annotations are very limited or unavailable, Training must be minimized (normal-only / few-shot / training-light preferred), Must run on single GPU or limited compute environment, Inspection time per source image must be under 1 minute, Must be robust to image style / contrast / noise level changes, End-to-end fine-tuning of very large foundation models is discouraged, Must avoid approaches that excessively increase false positives
input.target_metric: False Positive Rate on Normal Images, False Alarm per Image, Anomalous Area Ratio on Normal Images, Score Stability under Style/Noise Perturbation (Mean Absolute Score Shift and Normal Acceptance Rate under Perturbation), Threshold Robustness, Inference Time per Source Image, Peak Memory Usage, Throughput
